verses.text: Job answers that if his grief and calamity were weighed together, they would outweigh the sand of the sea. He pleads the sheer crushing weight of his pain. Before we judge a sufferer's words, we should reckon how heavy his burden truly is.

Job answers that if his grief and calamity were weighed together, they would outweigh the sand of the sea. He pleads the sheer crushing weight of his pain. Before we judge a sufferer's words, we should reckon how heavy his burden truly is.

Job answers that if his grief and calamity were weighed together, they would outweigh the sand of the sea. He pleads the sheer crushing weight of his pain. Before we judge a sufferer's words, we should reckon how heavy his burden truly is.

He feels 'the arrows of the Almighty' within him, their poison drinking up his spirit, so that he loathes his food. His distress is no mere complaint but real agony. There are afflictions of soul that take away all relish for life's plainest comforts.

He feels 'the arrows of the Almighty' within him, their poison drinking up his spirit, so that he loathes his food. His distress is no mere complaint but real agony. There are afflictions of soul that take away all relish for life's plainest comforts.

He feels 'the arrows of the Almighty' within him, their poison drinking up his spirit, so that he loathes his food. His distress is no mere complaint but real agony. There are afflictions of soul that take away all relish for life's plainest comforts.

Job's one request is that God would let him die, yet he comforts himself that he 'has not concealed the words of the Holy One.' Even longing for death, he clings to his integrity. A clear conscience is a treasure no suffering can take away.

'What is my strength, that I should hope?' he cries; his flesh is not brass nor his strength like stone. He owns he is at the end of himself. There comes a point where human endurance fails and only God can be the sufferer's stay.

'What is my strength, that I should hope?' he cries; his flesh is not brass nor his strength like stone. He owns he is at the end of himself. There comes a point where human endurance fails and only God can be the sufferer's stay.

'What is my strength, that I should hope?' he cries; his flesh is not brass nor his strength like stone. He owns he is at the end of himself. There comes a point where human endurance fails and only God can be the sufferer's stay.

Pity is due to the afflicted, he says, yet his friends have proved like a brook that dries up when the traveller most needs it. He laments their failed kindness. Few griefs sting like the cold comfort of friends who turn to critics.

Pity is due to the afflicted, he says, yet his friends have proved like a brook that dries up when the traveller most needs it. He laments their failed kindness. Few griefs sting like the cold comfort of friends who turn to critics.

Caravans turn aside to that brook and perish, ashamed because they had hoped in it. So Job's hopes in his friends are disappointed. To lean on man is to lean on what may fail us in the very hour of need.

'Ye are nothing,' he tells them; they see his ruin and are afraid, though he asked nothing of them — no gift, no rescue. Their fear has frozen their love. Job sought only sympathy, and even that they withheld.

'Ye are nothing,' he tells them; they see his ruin and are afraid, though he asked nothing of them — no gift, no rescue. Their fear has frozen their love. Job sought only sympathy, and even that they withheld.

'Ye are nothing,' he tells them; they see his ruin and are afraid, though he asked nothing of them — no gift, no rescue. Their fear has frozen their love. Job sought only sympathy, and even that they withheld.

'Teach me, and I will hold my tongue,' he says; 'how forcible are right words! but what doth your arguing reprove?' — they would even prey upon the helpless. He asks for proof, not mere reproof. Honest correction welcomes truth, but resents words that only wound.

'Teach me, and I will hold my tongue,' he says; 'how forcible are right words! but what doth your arguing reprove?' — they would even prey upon the helpless. He asks for proof, not mere reproof. Honest correction welcomes truth, but resents words that only wound.

'Look upon me,' Job urges; would he lie to their faces? 'Is there iniquity in my tongue?' He appeals to his own sincerity. A sufferer wrongly accused may rightly plead his innocence before God and men.
